title: Source Credibility Evaluator
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive tool where students evaluate environmental science claims by rating source expertise, evidence quality, transparency, and consistency

learningObjectives: Evaluate the credibility of environmental science claims by assessing source quality, methodology, and potential bias
Distinguish between peer-reviewed research, government reports, industry publications, and informal sources
Apply a systematic rubric for source evaluation
prerequisites: Basic understanding of the scientific method
Familiarity with common environmental science topics

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Gamified evaluation with scoring rubric and immediate feedback
keyQuestions: What makes a source credible in environmental science?
How can you identify potential bias in environmental claims?
Why is peer review important for scientific credibility?
How does funding source affect the trustworthiness of research?
commonMisconceptions: Students often assume that any published article is equally credible regardless of the source
Students may believe that government reports are always unbiased
Students sometimes think that the number of citations alone determines credibility
assessmentOpportunities: Track student scores across all 8 scenarios to identify patterns in evaluation skills
Have students apply the four-criteria rubric to real environmental news articles
Ask students to create their own claim-source pairs for classmates to evaluate
